title: Executive Order 9017—Establishing the National War Labor Board

flags.justification: The Board is a deliberately balanced tripartite structure (four public, four labor, four employer members) with a quorum rule requiring representation from every group (Sec. 1) — the design specifically prevents concentration in any one faction, and binding determination is reached only after negotiation and conciliation are exhausted (Sec. 3).
A clear multi-step process (direct negotiation, then conciliation, then Board certification and determination) with defined quorum rules and appointment structure.
Single coherent purpose: wartime labor dispute resolution.

The dispute-resolution process is concretely specified (mediation, voluntary arbitration, or Board-rule arbitration); the Board's own rule-making power (Sec. 4) fills in standard procedural detail rather than creating open-ended discretion.

The Board is the negotiated alternative to a voluntary no-strike/no-lockout agreement reached at a labor-industry conference (preamble) — a reciprocal arrangement, not a design that rewards behavior contrary to its purpose.

The Board is explicitly created 'for the duration of the war' (preamble) — a concrete, war-tied natural end-state, distinct from the open-ended 'national emergency as declared on [date]' framing used elsewhere in this era's orders, which persisted indefinitely in practice.

Sec. 7 explicitly preserves the Railway Labor Act, National Labor Relations Act, Fair Labor Standards Act, and related statutes; Sec. 6 formally succeeds the prior National Defense Mediation Board rather than removing any oversight.
The multi-step process gives Department of Labor conciliators and the tripartite Board itself multiple, balanced institutional checkpoints before a binding determination is reached.
The order's text is bounded to wartime labor disputes affecting war production; it does not claim authority or establish a definitional framework reusable beyond that stated scope.

Sec. 6 is an explicit, clean succession — the prior National Defense Mediation Board (EO 8716) ceases to exist and its employees, records, and funds transfer to the new Board — not an ad hoc redirection of an unrelated agency's mandate.

Sec. 2's deference to disputes 'for which procedures for adjustment or settlement are otherwise provided' is a narrow, sensible carve-out for already-covered industries with their own established channels, not a broad exemption undermining the Board's purpose.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 0/22 = 0.0%. One of the most historically consequential WWII labor institutions, and a clean illustration that structural weight and historical significance are genuinely distinct measures — the tripartite, checks-and-balances design scores cleanly across every flag.
